project.name: AzTracker
project.description: Cloudflare Workers-based Amazon.eg price tracking engine with D1 SQL database, KV namespaces, queues, Telegram bot integration with bilingual i18n (English/Egyptian Arabic), AIMD governor for scraping frequency, and circuit breaker pattern.

nodes.name: db.js
nodes.summary: Database access layer providing cached user role resolution (rootAdmin, admin, approved), Telegram profile lookup with caching, audit logging with retry logic, and database cleanup utilities for the D1 database.

nodes.summary: Telegram webhook handler processing incoming messages and callbacks, implementing rate limiting, product tracking workflows, main menu rendering, user name syncing, and Mini App message sending for the Amazon.eg price tracker bot.

nodes.name: cron_trigger.js
nodes.summary: Cloudflare Worker scheduled cron handler that triggers product scraping, pauses subscriptions for dormant users, rejects idle unapproved users, cleans up expired bot state, and tracks hardware cron intervals.
nodes.languageNotes: Parses cron expression to detect hardware cron intervals. Uses MESSAGE_QUEUE and SCRAPER_QUEUE for async task dispatch.

nodes.label: src/workers/scraper_engine.js
nodes.description: Core price scraping engine with deal detection and alert dispatch. Fetches live Amazon product prices via the Creators API, computes time-weighted EMA statistics (30-day half-life), detects price drops/restocks/target matches, queues Telegram alerts via Cloudflare Queues, and persists price history to KV. Includes circuit breaker, anti-flap timers, and broadcast collection for public channel.
nodes.title: Scraper Engine
nodes.languageNotes: Implements statistical analysis (time-weighted mean/variance with Bessel's correction, z-score), exponential moving average with 30-day half-life decay, circuit breaker (5 failures / 5-min cooldown), anti-flap hysteresis (1-hour grace + 1 EGP debounce), and 2-phase commit (queue dispatch before DB batch).
nodes.name: src/workers/scraper_engine.js
nodes.summary: file node: src/workers/scraper_engine.js

nodes.label: checkCircuitBreaker(env)
nodes.description: Reads the Amazon API circuit breaker state from KV. Returns 'open', 'half_open', or 'closed'. If open and the 5-minute cooldown has elapsed, transitions to half_open. On KV read failure, defaults to closed (fail-open).
nodes.title: checkCircuitBreaker

nodes.description: Resets the circuit breaker to closed state with zero failures. Non-blocking (errors silently swallowed). Used after a successful API call when the breaker was in half_open state.
nodes.title: recordCircuitSuccess
nodes.name: recordCircuitSuccess
nodes.summary: function node: recordCircuitSuccess

nodes.label: recordCircuitFailure(env)
nodes.description: Increments the consecutive failure counter in KV. If failures reach the threshold (5), transitions the circuit breaker to open state and records the timestamp. Non-blocking on KV errors.
nodes.title: recordCircuitFailure
nodes.name: recordCircuitFailure

nodes.name: queue_worker.js
nodes.summary: Cloudflare Queues consumer that drives two pipelines: a paginated scraper fan-out on the scraper-queue and a Telegram alert delivery loop on the message-queue with rate-limit handling, bot-block detection, atomic D1 lock updates, and dead-lettering.
nodes.languageNotes: Two-queue dispatch by batch.queue prefix. Telegram branch implements 429 rate-limit backoff, 403 bot-block auto-pause via D1 UPDATE on User_Subscriptions, and a 2-phase-commit pattern where the D1 alert_sent_* flag is only flipped after a successful Telegram API call. Exceeded-retry messages are written to Failed_Queue_Messages for replay.

nodes.name: deploy_worker.yml
nodes.summary: GitHub Actions workflow that deploys the AzTracker Cloudflare Worker and applies D1 migrations on pushes to main, dev, and feature branches. Applies migrations and deploys separately for development (dev/feature) and production (main) environments using Wrangler v4.92.0.
nodes.languageNotes: Uses environment-conditional job steps: dev and feature branches target the development D1 database, while main targets production. Pinned to Node 22 and Wrangler 4.92.0 for reproducibility.

nodes.name: sync-prod-to-dev.yml
nodes.summary: On-demand GitHub Actions workflow that copies production D1 data and KV namespace contents into the development environment. Exports production rows as SQL, rewrites INSERT into INSERT OR REPLACE to avoid constraint violations, then replays against the dev database.
nodes.languageNotes: Manual workflow_dispatch trigger only — not tied to git events. Relies on sed transformation of the exported SQL to safely merge rows into dev without schema recreation. Delegates KV synchronization to scripts/sync-kv.js.

nodes.label: 0000_baseline.sql
nodes.summary: Baseline D1 SQLite schema for AzTracker Phase 6.8. Creates 8 tables (Users, Join_Queue, Global_Products, User_Subscriptions, Audit_Logs, Bot_States, Failed_Queue_Messages) with 13 performance indexes. Fully idempotent with IF NOT EXISTS guards.
nodes.description: Foundational database migration that establishes the complete relational schema for the AzTracker price tracking engine. Defines the identity/access directory (Users), join queue for pending access requests (Join_Queue), the global hysteresis and state engine for product tracking (Global_Products), the junction table linking users to tracked products (User_Subscriptions), audit logging (Audit_Logs), bot conversational state offload (Bot_States), and dead letter queue for failed queue messages (Failed_Queue_Messages). Includes 13 performance indexes optimized for CRM queries, cron engine joins, garbage collection, and audit log filtering.
nodes.name: migrations/0000_baseline.sql

nodes.label: 0001_add_last_active.sql
nodes.summary: Adds last_active column to Users table for dormancy sweep tracking. Backfills existing users with their created_at timestamp to prevent premature sweeping.
nodes.description: Schema migration that introduces a last_active INTEGER column (default 0) on the Users table. This enables the dormancy sweep mechanism to identify and clean up inactive users. Includes a backfill statement that sets last_active to created_at for all existing users where last_active is 0 or NULL, ensuring existing users are not immediately swept.
nodes.name: migrations/0001_add_last_active.sql

nodes.label: 0002_add_always_track.sql
nodes.summary: Adds always_track column to Global_Products, enabling admins to flag orphaned products for global deal hunting even with zero active user subscriptions.
nodes.description: Schema migration that introduces an always_track INTEGER column (default 0) on the Global_Products table. This flag allows administrators to mark products that should continue to be tracked by the cron engine for deal detection even when no user has an active subscription for that product, preventing orphaned products from being garbage collected.
nodes.name: migrations/0002_add_always_track.sql

nodes.label: 0003_add_mute_queue.sql
nodes.summary: Adds mute_join_queue column to Users table, allowing admins to opt out of Telegram join queue notifications.
nodes.description: Schema migration that introduces a mute_join_queue INTEGER column (default 0) on the Users table. This allows administrators to suppress Telegram notifications for new join requests to the system. Default value of 0 means admins receive notifications by default.
nodes.name: migrations/0003_add_mute_queue.sql

nodes.label: 0004_add_detail_page_url.sql
nodes.summary: Adds detail_page_url column to Global_Products to store the canonical affiliate URL from the Amazon Creators API.
nodes.description: Schema migration that introduces a detail_page_url TEXT column on the Global_Products table. This stores the full canonical affiliate URL (including tag, marketplace, and language parameters) from the Amazon Creators API, eliminating the need to manually construct product URLs and ensuring accurate affiliate linking.
nodes.name: migrations/0004_add_detail_page_url.sql

nodes.label: Users
nodes.summary: Identity and access directory table. Stores Telegram user identity, language preference, role-based access control, item limits, ban status, and notification preferences.
nodes.description: Core user table with chat_id as primary key. Tracks first_name, username, language (en/ar), role (approved/admin/rejected/pending), item_limit, approval metadata, ban status (unban_rejected), and notification preferences (mute_join_queue, last_active). Referenced by User_Subscriptions via foreign key with CASCADE delete.
nodes.name: Users

nodes.label: Join_Queue
nodes.summary: Pending access requests and unban appeals queue. Stores join request metadata including request type and admin message history.
nodes.description: Queue table for pending user access requests and unban appeals. Uses chat_id as primary key. Tracks request_type (access/unban), requested timestamp, admin_messages for conversation history, and language inherited from Telegram settings.
nodes.name: Join_Queue

nodes.label: Global_Products
nodes.summary: The global hysteresis and state engine. Tracks product pricing (new/used/Amazon), seller data, hysteresis timestamps for anti-flap protection, historical statistics, broadcast locks, and affiliate URLs.
nodes.description: Central product tracking table with asin as primary key. Stores bilingual product names (name, name_ar), image URL, live pricing for new/used/Amazon conditions with seller and marketplace IDs, hysteresis timestamps (seen_amazon_eg_at, seen_resale_at, missing_since fields), state flags (delisted, is_atl_new, always_track), historical math (hist_mean, hist_stdev), broadcast locks (last_broadcast_time_ms, last_broadcast_price), and the canonical affiliate detail_page_url. Referenced by User_Subscriptions via foreign key with CASCADE delete.
nodes.name: Global_Products

nodes.label: User_Subscriptions
nodes.summary: Junction table linking users to tracked products. Stores per-user target prices, pause state, and alert delivery flags.
nodes.description: Many-to-many junction table with composite primary key (chat_id, asin). Foreign keys to Users and Global_Products with CASCADE delete. Tracks target_price, is_paused status, alert_sent flags for new and used conditions, added_at timestamp, and paused_at timestamp.
nodes.name: User_Subscriptions

nodes.label: Audit_Logs
nodes.summary: Audit logging table for tracking administrative and system actions with actor identification and target references.
nodes.description: Append-only audit log with auto-incrementing id. Records timestamp, actor_id, actor_name, action type, optional target_id, and details text. Indexed for chronological queries and per-actor filtering.
nodes.name: Audit_Logs

nodes.label: Bot_States
nodes.summary: Bot conversational state table for D1 offload. Stores key-value pairs with expiration for temporary bot state management.
nodes.description: Key-value store for bot conversation state persisted in D1. Uses a string key as primary key, stores a string value, and an expires_at timestamp for garbage collection of stale state entries.
nodes.name: Bot_States

nodes.label: Failed_Queue_Messages

Records queue_name, message body, number of attempts, last error message, and failure timestamp. Enables debugging and potential retry of failed async operations such as Telegram alert delivery.

nodes.label: README.md
nodes.summary: Comprehensive project documentation for AzTracker, a serverless Amazon.eg price tracking engine built on Cloudflare Workers, D1, and KV. Documents architecture, engineering achievements, environment variables, CI/CD pipeline, and acknowledgements.
nodes.description: Serves as the primary project entry point and living documentation. Describes the full system: a multi-tenant price tracking architecture with dual-hysteresis anti-flap protection, bilingual (English/Egyptian Arabic) edge-rendered CRM, EMA-based deal detection, decoupled async Telegram alerts via Cloudflare Queues, dynamic governor queue scheduling, and omnichannel deep-links. Includes architecture pipeline (Mermaid diagram), modular ES6 directory structure, environment variable tables (plaintext vars, Cloudflare secrets, GitHub repo secrets, Cloudflare bindings), and CI/CD database synchronization details.
nodes.title: AzTracker Project README
nodes.languageNotes: Written in English with project-specific technical terms: SIEM auditing, Two-Phase Commit (2PC), exponential moving average (EMA), dual-hysteresis anti-flap, dynamic governor, queue consumers/producers, edge-rendered, omnichannel deep-links, persistent menu.
nodes.languageLesson: The README uses a modern open-source documentation style with embedded HTML tables, Mermaid architecture diagrams, badge shields, and anchor-link navigation. It mixes technical specifications with feature highlights, serving both as an onboarding guide and an architectural reference.
nodes.name: readme

nodes.label: package.json

nodes.description: Standard npm package manifest. Sets the main entry to src/index.js, specifies a 'doc' directory alias, defines a placeholder test script, and registers the project as a public ISC-licensed repository on GitHub (aka-khalid/AzTracker). Dev dependencies include @cloudflare/workers-types (for TypeScript type definitions targeting the Cloudflare Workers runtime), Puppeteer (likely used for E2E or scraper testing), and TypeScript (for type-checking without compilation since the Worker runs native ES6 JS).
nodes.title: AzTracker Package Manifest
nodes.languageNotes: JSON format. Notable: the 'type' field is set to 'commonjs' despite the Worker using ES modules — this may govern local tooling behavior. The version field is 1.0.0. The description field contains an HTML div artifact, which appears to be a copy-paste error from the README.
nodes.languageLesson: The package.json illustrates a Cloudflare Workers project that uses TypeScript only for type-checking (via @cloudflare/workers-types) without a compile step, since Workers natively execute ES modules. Puppeteer is included as a dev dependency for browser-based testing of the edge-rendered CRM interfaces.
nodes.name: package-json

nodes.label: wrangler.toml
nodes.summary: Cloudflare Workers deployment configuration for AzTracker. Defines dual-environment (dev/production) wiring for D1 databases, KV namespaces, queue producers/consumers, Cron triggers, and environment variables.
nodes.description: The Wrangler configuration file orchestrates the complete Cloudflare infrastructure. Dev environment: binds KV namespace AZTRACKER_DB, D1 database 'aztracker-dev-db', two queue producers (MESSAGE_QUEUE -> telegram-outbox-dev, SCRAPER_QUEUE -> scraper-queue-dev) with matching consumers (batch size 30/10 respectively, max retries 3, 1s timeout), environment vars (GITHUB_OWNER/REPO, product limit 3, daily queue limit 10000, channel @AzTrackerr, bot username AzTrackerr_bot). Production environment mirrors dev with prod IDs, real queue names (telegram-outbox, scraper-queue), and an active cron trigger (* * * * * every minute). Uses smart placement mode. Mirations directory is 'migrations'. Cron trigger is currently disabled in dev (empty array).
nodes.title: Wrangler Deployment Configuration
nodes.languageNotes: TOML format with Cloudflare Wrangler-specific schema. Key wrangler concepts: kv_namespaces (binding + id), d1_databases (binding + database_name + database_id + migrations_dir), queues.producers (binding + queue), queues.consumers (queue + batching/retries settings), triggers.crons, [vars] for plaintext env, and [env.production] for environment-specific overrides.
nodes.languageLesson: The toml file demonstrates Cloudflare's multi-environment pattern: a default (dev) configuration with an [env.production] section that inherits and overrides specific values (name, var IDs, cron schedules). Queue consumers are configured with batch_size (parallelism), max_batch_timeout (latency vs throughput tradeoff), and max_retries (failure resilience) — critical parameters for the 2PC alert delivery system described in the README.
nodes.name: wrangler-toml

nodes.description: Provides complete deployment instructions for the AzTracker Cloudflare Workers project. Documents the dual-environment pattern (aztracker-dev-worker / aztracker-prod-worker) with separate D1 databases but shared KV namespace. Describes the V2 modular ES6 source structure under src/ (core/, routes/, workers/). Includes step-by-step provisioning commands (D1 database creation, schema migrations), Cloudflare secret injection (TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN, AMAZON_CLIENT_ID, etc.), GitHub Actions repository secret configuration (CLOUDFLARE_ACCOUNT_ID, CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN), deployment commands for both environments, the 'Sync Prod to Dev' GitHub Action for database synchronization, Telegram webhook registration via curl, and the persistent menu setup script (setup_bot_commands.js) that must be run for both dev and prod bot tokens.

nodes.label: docs/ROADMAP.md
nodes.summary: Comprehensive architecture roadmap and technical debt tracker documenting completed Phase 6 milestones (6.8 through 6.16), planned Phase 7 R&D items, and intentional architectural boundaries. Covers D1 migration, async queues, bilingual localization, CRM overhaul, security hardening, deal detection, database synchronization, and i18n hardening.
nodes.description: The definitive architectural decision log for AzTracker, organized by numbered phases. Completed Phase 6 milestones: 6.8 (Native D1 Migration & UI Overhaul - migrated from KV to relational D1, built interactive Web App CRM), 6.10 (Monolith Decomposition & Async Queues - split 2500+ line worker into modular ES6 architecture, Creators API migration, Two-Phase Commit outbox pattern), 6.11 (Professional Masry Localization & RTL Engine - i18n dictionary engine with 233 keys, RTL CSS logical properties, per-user language detection, organic broadcast templates), 6.13 Two entries (Legacy Interface Deprecation - removed text commands; Visual CRM Overhaul - product images, Hot Deals tab, EMA deal detection, tiered price buckets, deep-links, secure fetchAPI wrapper, GitHub Actions D1 sync, safe SQL transformation, KV mirroring), 6.12 (Telegram Web App & Resilient Fallback Scraping - edge-rendered HTML dashboard, HMAC-SHA256 initData validation, dynamic affiliate injection, HTTP language scraping, Arabic cross-pollination fix), 6.14 Two entries (Statistical Engine Overhaul - time-weighted integrals, Z-score, 7-day failsafe, Abandoned Products Hub, Keep Alive toggle, interactive chart intervals; Persistent Menu & Edge Navigation Resilience - database as source of truth, navigation loop resolution, graceful 403 handling, secure loading overlay, cross-browser hardening), 6.15 (Product Variations CRM Broadcasting - child variation caching, interactive broadcast picker, zero-cost template generation, RTL layout hardening with Unicode RLE), 6.16 (Native Confirm Dialogs, Toast Notifications & i18n Hardening - custom showConfirmDialog modal, green success toast, image fallback hardening, i18n key additions, locale cleanup removing Fusha). Phase 7 planned: Multi-Region Support (amazon.sa, amazon.ae). Intentional boundaries: no Docker/K8s, no React/Next.js, no synchronous CRM history loading, no percentage-based target pricing. V1 archive documents Python engine phases 1-5.
nodes.title: AzTracker Architecture Roadmap & Tech Debt
nodes.languageNotes: Written in English with embedded Arabic UI strings (e.g., 'منتجات مهجورة' for Abandoned Products, '🔥 عروض نار' for Hot Deals, 'الحق العرض من هنا' for affiliate CTA). The document intentionally archives V1 Python history for context while clearly delineating the V2 JavaScript architecture. The 'Intentional Architectural Boundaries' section is particularly valuable -- it documents not just what was built, but what was explicitly rejected and why, providing crucial context for future contributors.

nodes.label: docs/TESTING.md
nodes.summary: Test specification for AzTracker's asynchronous queue architecture, covering scraper queue pagination, Telegram outbox rate limiting, localization/CRM integration, and native confirm dialogs/toast notifications. Uses Edge-Diagnostic Test Vectors instead of heavy E2E frameworks.
nodes.description: Living test specification organized into four test categories. Category 1 - Async Queue Architecture (TC-101 to TC-103): validates recursive pagination of the scraper queue (offset-based batching, hasMore flag, delaySeconds:1 chaining), scraper API outage handling (failsafe abort with retry delaySeconds:30), and pagination termination (final batch with hasMore=false). Category 2 - Telegram Outbox & Rate Limits (TC-201 to TC-204): validates asynchronous queueing of alert payloads to MESSAGE_QUEUE, HTTP 429 backoff handling (rateLimited flag, retry_after), HTTP 403 auto-pause of user subscriptions, and atomic Two-Phase Commit (alert_sent_new/alert_sent_used flags only committed after HTTP 200). Includes note about DLQ (Dead Letter Queue) fallback in queue_worker.js and max_retries configuration in wrangler.toml. Category 3 - Localization & CRM Integration (TC-301 to TC-303): validates bilingual Arabic alert rendering with currency symbols, RTL dashboard routing with dir='rtl' and lang='masry', and CRM action endpoint with HMAC validation. Category 4 - Native Confirm Dialogs & Toast Notifications (TC-401 to TC-411): validates custom showConfirmDialog modal rendering, stacking guard, keyboard support (Enter/Escape), RTL button order, green success toast, user dashboard confirm dialog, image fallback placeholder, i18n search placeholder, subscribers drawer title, and locale dictionary integrity (no Fusha keys). Execution protocol uses wrangler queues send and wrangler tail for diagnostics.
nodes.title: AzTracker Test Suite & Diagnostic Vectors
nodes.languageNotes: Written in English with test case IDs (TC-101 through TC-411), structured as Markdown tables with Test ID / Feature Target / Diagnostic Vector / Expected Success Criterion columns. Uses Cloudflare-specific tooling commands (wrangler queues send, wrangler tail, wrangler d1 execute). Key testing concepts: Edge-Diagnostic Test Vectors (lightweight mock payloads injected into queues), Two-Phase Commit verification, DLQ (Dead Letter Queue) fallback, max_retries configuration. References specific source files (scraper_engine.js, queue_worker.js, crm_dashboard.js, i18n.js) and database tables (User_Subscriptions).
nodes.languageLesson: The testing document reveals AzTracker's pragmatic testing philosophy: instead of heavy E2E frameworks that would drain KV/D1 allocations, it uses lightweight diagnostic vectors injected directly into Cloudflare Queues. This approach tests the actual production code paths (real queue consumers, real D1 queries) without the overhead of a test harness. The test cases are designed to be executed sequentially using wrangler CLI commands, with results verified via wrangler tail (log streaming) and direct D1 queries. The document also documents an important configuration subtlety: the DLQ logic in queue_worker.js is natively unreachable because wrangler.toml caps max_retries=3, requiring temporary config changes to test DLQ paths.

keySections: Telegram Outbox & Rate Limits (TC-201 to TC-204)
Native Confirm Dialogs & Toast Notifications (TC-401 to TC-411)

nodes.label: crm_dashboard.js
nodes.summary: Admin CRM dashboard API and HTML renderer. Handles user management (approve/revoke/unban/promote/demote), product management (graveyard, paused, active, top-charts), audit logging, broadcast messaging, live price fetching with circuit breaker, KV-to-D1 migration, and GitHub env sync. Renders a full Tailwind CSS single-page admin app with inline JS.

nodes.label: getAmazonAccessToken
nodes.summary: Obtains an OAuth2 client_credentials access token from Amazon API using clientId and clientSecret. Returns the access_token string.

nodes.label: AmazonEdgeParser
nodes.summary: Class wrapping the Amazon Creators API. Methods: getItems (batch price/condition/seller fetch), getItemsWithArabic (Arabic title via languagesOfPreference), getVariations (child ASIN lookup), scrapeArabicTitle (HTML fallback), scrapeEnglishTitle (HTML fallback), parseItem (normalize listings into price/seller/condition fields).

nodes.label: escapeHtml
nodes.summary: Escapes &, <, >, ", and ' characters in a string for safe HTML insertion.

nodes.label: formatEGP
nodes.summary: Formats a numeric price with comma-separated thousands and 2 decimal places for EGP display.

nodes.label: delay
nodes.summary: Returns a Promise that resolves after the specified number of milliseconds.

nodes.label: expandAmazonUrl
nodes.summary: Follows up to 3 redirect hops to resolve Amazon short URLs (amzn.to, amzn.eu, a.co, amazon.eg/d/) to full product URLs.

nodes.label: getAsinFromUrl
nodes.summary: Extracts a 10-character ASIN from Amazon /dp/ or /gp/product/ URL patterns.

nodes.label: truncateName
nodes.summary: Truncates a product name to a maximum length, appending '...' if exceeded.

nodes.label: resolveProductName
nodes.summary: Resolves the display name for a product item, preferring Arabic name when lang is 'masry'.

nodes.label: convertHindiToArabic
nodes.summary: Converts Eastern Arabic-Indic digits (U+0660-U+0669) to Western ASCII digits (0-9).

nodes.label: getCairoTime
nodes.summary: Formats a timestamp as a Cairo-timezone (EET) date-time string.

nodes.label: buildBroadcastMessage
nodes.summary: Builds a Telegram broadcast message (text + inline_keyboard) for a deal, with two-tier header logic based on price drop percentage thresholds.

nodes.label: fetchAPI
nodes.summary: CRM API router handling CORS preflight, history retrieval, live price fetch with circuit breaker, broadcast text generation, ASIN testing, KV-to-D1 migration, audit log queries, user/product data, and admin actions (force_scrape, broadcast, approve, reject, revoke, unban, promote, demote, set_limit, etc.).

nodes.label: renderCrmHTML
nodes.summary: Generates the complete CRM dashboard HTML page with Tailwind CSS styling, Telegram WebApp integration, Chart.js, and inline JavaScript for admin management UI (system view, users tab, audit tab, product drawers, broadcast composer).

nodes.label: checkCircuitBreaker
nodes.summary: Reads the Amazon API circuit breaker state from KV. Returns 'closed', 'open', or 'half_open'. Transitions from open to half_open after the cooldown period elapses.

nodes.label: recordCircuitSuccess
nodes.summary: Resets the circuit breaker state to 'closed' with zero failures in KV.

nodes.label: recordCircuitFailure
nodes.summary: Increments the failure counter in KV and opens the circuit if the threshold (5 consecutive failures) is reached.

nodes.label: executeScrapeEngine
nodes.summary: Main scrape batch processor. Queries D1 for stale products, fetches live prices from Amazon Creators API, enriches Arabic/English names, computes time-weighted statistics, detects price drops and target hits, dispatches Telegram alerts via MESSAGE_QUEUE, and broadcasts best deals to the public channel.

nodes.name: index.js
nodes.summary: Main Cloudflare Worker entry point. Exports scheduled (Cron), queue (Queue Worker), and fetch (HTTP) handlers. Implements IP-based rate limiting per route group (CRM, user, test-asin) and routes requests to appropriate handlers.
nodes.languageNotes: Central orchestrator that re-exports handlers from route and worker modules. Rate limiting uses an in-memory Map with a 60-second sliding window and 50k entry safety valve.

nodes.name: checkIPRateLimit
nodes.summary: IP-based sliding-window rate limiter. Tracks request count per IP with a 1-minute window. Returns true if request is within limits. Includes a safety valve that clears the map if it exceeds 50,000 entries.

nodes.name: ROADMAP.md
nodes.summary: Architecture roadmap and tech debt tracker documenting completed Phase 6 milestones (6.8 through 6.16) including D1 migration, async queue pipeline, Masry localization, Web App CRM, visual overhaul, native dialogs, and product variations broadcasting. Also covers intentional architectural boundaries and archived V1 Python engine history.
nodes.languageNotes: Detailed execution briefs for each completed phase with checkboxes, covering statistical engine overhaul, EMA deal detection, i18n hardening, and security improvements.

nodes.name: t
nodes.summary: Core translation function: looks up a flat key in the dictionary, normalizes Arabic variants to 'masry', applies {placeholder} interpolation, and falls back to the key itself on miss.

nodes.name: getWelcomeMessage
nodes.summary: Assembles the full multi-step welcome message for new users by concatenating translated heading, 5 onboarding steps, a pro tip, and the Amazon affiliate disclaimer.

nodes.name: fetchUserAPI
nodes.summary: Authenticates Telegram WebApp initData via HMAC-SHA256, enforces IP-based rate limits, and routes API requests for product CRUD operations (list, add, remove, toggle pause, update target price) against D1.

nodes.name: renderUserHTML
nodes.summary: Renders the full interactive user dashboard HTML page (~780 lines) with embedded CSS/JS, localized product cards, price history, subscription management controls, and WebApp initialization. The largest single function in the codebase.
nodes.languageNotes: Generates a complete single-page HTML application with inline CSS and JS, including RTL support for Arabic.

nodes.name: resolveLanguageCode
nodes.summary: Maps a Telegram language_code string to a supported i18n language: any Arabic variant maps to 'masry', English to 'en', and everything else defaults to 'masry'.

nodes.name: checkUserRateLimit
nodes.summary: IP-based rate limiter (60 req/min) for the user dashboard WebApp using an in-memory Map with a 50k-entry safety valve.

nodes.name: constructor
nodes.summary: Initializes the AmazonEdgeParser with an access token and optional locale for API requests.

nodes.name: getItems
nodes.summary: Fetches product items from Amazon Edge API for a list of ASINs. Returns parsed product data including prices, availability, and seller information.

nodes.name: getItemsWithArabic
nodes.summary: Fetches product items with Arabic locale support, returning product data with Arabic-language titles and descriptions.

nodes.name: getVariations
nodes.summary: Retrieves product variations (size, color, etc.) for a given ASIN from Amazon.

nodes.name: scrapeArabicTitle
nodes.summary: Scrapes the Arabic title for a product from its detail page when not available via API.

nodes.name: scrapeEnglishTitle
nodes.summary: Scrapes the English title for a product from its detail page when not available via API.

nodes.name: parseItem
nodes.summary: Parses a raw Amazon Edge API item response into a normalized product data object with price, availability, seller, and buy box information.

nodes.name: escapeHtml
nodes.summary: Escapes HTML special characters in a string to prevent XSS attacks in rendered output.

nodes.name: formatEGP
nodes.summary: Formats a numeric value as Egyptian Pound (EGP) currency string with proper locale formatting.

nodes.name: delay
nodes.summary: Returns a promise that resolves after a specified number of milliseconds. Used for rate limiting between API requests.

nodes.name: expandAmazonUrl
nodes.summary: Expands a shortened Amazon URL to its full canonical form.

nodes.name: getAsinFromUrl
nodes.summary: Extracts the ASIN (Amazon Standard Identification Number) from various Amazon URL formats.

nodes.name: truncateName
nodes.summary: Truncates a product name to a maximum length, adding ellipsis if truncated.

nodes.name: resolveProductName
nodes.summary: Resolves the best display name for a product, preferring Arabic name when available, falling back to English.

nodes.name: convertHindiToArabic
nodes.summary: Converts Hindi/Indic numerals to Arabic numerals for consistent display in Arabic-language contexts.

nodes.name: getCairoTime
nodes.summary: Returns the current time formatted for the Africa/Cairo timezone.

nodes.name: buildBroadcastMessage
nodes.summary: Constructs a formatted broadcast message string for sending to Telegram users.

nodes.name: generateSignature
nodes.summary: Generates an HMAC signature for Telegram initData verification to authenticate admin requests.

nodes.name: verifyInitData
nodes.summary: Verifies Telegram WebApp initData by checking the hash signature and data freshness. Returns parsed user data if valid.

nodes.name: authAdmin
nodes.summary: Authenticates an admin user by verifying Telegram initData and checking admin privileges in the database.

nodes.name: fetchAPI
nodes.summary: Main API handler for CRM dashboard requests. Handles product lookup, user management, join queue approval/rejection, product deletion, global product CRUD, force scrape triggering, and broadcast messaging. Contains extensive business logic for the admin interface.

nodes.name: renderCrmHTML
nodes.summary: Generates the complete CRM dashboard HTML page with embedded CSS and JavaScript. Renders the full admin interface including product management tables, user lists, join queue, broadcast composer, and statistics panels as a single server-side rendered response.

nodes.name: checkCircuitBreaker
nodes.summary: Checks the circuit breaker state from KV storage. Returns the current state (CLOSED, OPEN, HALF_OPEN) and whether requests should be allowed through.

nodes.name: recordCircuitSuccess
nodes.summary: Records a successful API call by resetting the circuit breaker state to CLOSED in KV storage.

nodes.name: recordCircuitFailure
nodes.summary: Records a failed API call by incrementing the failure count, potentially opening the circuit breaker, and implementing exponential backoff for the retry window.

nodes.name: truncateMessage
nodes.summary: Truncates a Telegram message to the maximum allowed length (4096 chars), breaking at the last newline boundary.

nodes.name: executeScrapeEngine
nodes.summary: Main scraper engine entry point. Fetches stale products from D1, checks circuit breaker, obtains Amazon access token, scrapes product prices via AmazonEdgeParser, evaluates price alerts against user subscriptions using statistical analysis (mean, stdev, exponential smoothing), sends Telegram notifications, and updates product records in D1.

edges.description: Access tokens acquired via getAmazonAccessToken are used to construct AmazonEdgeParser instances and authorize their Creators API calls.
getItems iterates over the API response and calls parseItem for each raw item to produce normalized results.
getVariations enriches its raw variation data by calling getItems (which internally calls parseItem) for each chunk of child ASINs.
scrapeArabicTitle serves as a fallback when getItemsWithArabic returns no Arabic title from the Creators API.
scrapeEnglishTitle is a fallback path for English titles when the Creators API does not return them.
getVariations calls getItems in batches of 10 to enrich raw variation data with accurate pricing.
buildBroadcastMessage calls escapeHtml to sanitize product names and seller names before embedding them in Telegram HTML.
buildBroadcastMessage calls formatEGP to render the deal price in Egyptian pounds.
buildBroadcastMessage calls truncateName to constrain the product name length in the broadcast text.
buildBroadcastMessage calls getCairoTime to append a localized timestamp to the broadcast.
buildBroadcastMessage uses resolveProductName to pick the Arabic product name when available.
Both functions handle Amazon URL parsing: getAsinFromUrl extracts ASINs while expandAmazonUrl resolves short links. They are typically used together in URL-processing pipelines.
fetchAPI calls checkCircuitBreaker before making Amazon API calls in the /api/crm/live-price endpoint to respect the circuit breaker state.
fetchAPI calls recordCircuitSuccess via ctx.waitWhen the circuit breaker is half_open and a live-price call succeeds.
fetchAPI calls recordCircuitFailure via ctx.waitWhen a live-price call fails with an API error.
fetchAPI calls authAdmin at the start of protected API endpoints to verify Telegram WebApp authentication and resolve admin roles.
authAdmin calls verifyInitData to validate the Telegram WebApp init data HMAC signature from the Authorization header.
fetchAPI defines and calls resolveTargetLang to localize outbound Telegram notifications when performing user actions (approve, reject, revoke, promote, demote, etc.).
fetchAPI defines and calls adminLangPref to localize callback query responses sent back to the acting admin.
The inline JavaScript in renderCrmHTML calls fetchAPI (the client-side wrapper, not the server-side handler) to communicate with the CRM backend endpoints.
checkCircuitBreaker transitions to half_open after cooldown; recordCircuitSuccess is called after a successful API call in half_open state to close the breaker.
executeScrapeEngine calls checkCircuitBreaker at the start of each batch to skip scraping when the Amazon API circuit is open.
executeScrapeEngine calls recordCircuitSuccess when the circuit breaker is half_open and the API call succeeds.
executeScrapeEngine calls recordCircuitFailure when the Amazon API throws an error, incrementing the failure counter.
executeScrapeEngine calls truncateMessage to enforce the 4096-character Telegram limit on alert texts before queuing.
executeScrapeEngine calls queueAlert for each detected deal (price drop, restock, target match) to build and enqueue Telegram alert messages.

layers.name: Entry Point
layers.description: Root modules — main entry, cron trigger, and queue worker entry handlers. These are the Cloudflare Worker export surfaces (scheduled, queue, fetch) that receive platform events and delegate inward.

layers.name: Route Handlers
layers.description: HTTP request routing and edge-rendered UI. CRM dashboard (admin), user dashboard (Hot Deals discovery), and Telegram webhook (message/callback processing). All three import core services and the scraper engine.

layers.name: Core Services
layers.description: Business logic and external service integrations with no upward dependencies. Amazon Creators API (product data), Telegram Bot API (messaging), D1 database access (CRUD, roles, audit), i18n engine (EN/AR-Masry bilingual), and shared utilities (formatting, parsing, escaping).

layers.name: Async Workers
layers.description: Background task processing engine. Scraper engine fetches prices from Amazon, computes EMA deal detection, hysteresis anti-flap, and dynamic price buckets. Consumed by route handlers and the queue worker.

layers.name: Operations
layers.description: Operational tooling and scripts for bot setup, KV synchronization, secret provisioning, screenshot generation, and schema seeding. Not part of the deployed worker runtime.

layers.name: Infrastructure
layers.description: Cloudflare platform configuration (wrangler.toml: KV, D1, Queues bindings, triggers), CI/CD pipelines (deploy worker, sync prod-to-dev), and Node.js package manifest.

layers.name: Data Layer
layers.description: D1 SQL migration files defining the relational schema — baseline tables, last_active, always_track, mute_queue, and detail_page_url columns.

layers.name: Documentation
layers.description: Project documentation: architecture README, deployment guide, visual gallery (21 LTR/RTL screenshots), engineering roadmap, and test specification.

tour.title: Project Overview
tour.description: Start with README.md to understand what AzTracker is and why it exists. The README describes a highly scalable, multi-tenant Amazon.eg price tracking engine built on Cloudflare Workers, D1 SQL, and Queues. It features a localized (English/Egyptian Arabic) interactive Admin CRM Web App, dual-hysteresis anti-flap protection, and dynamic queue-based scheduling. This document sets the stage for every code file you will encounter in the tour.

tour.title: Worker Entry Point
tour.description: The Cloudflare Worker entry point (src/index.js) is where every HTTP request begins. It imports and routes to the three main route handlers -- telegram_webhook, user_dashboard, and crm_dashboard -- and directly calls into the database and Telegram core modules. With a fan-out of 5 edges, this file has the broadest scope in the codebase and serves as the top-level orchestrator for all incoming requests.

tour.title: Route Handlers -- Webhook and Dashboards
tour.description: The three route handlers define the HTTP interface of the application. telegram_webhook.js receives and processes incoming Telegram Bot messages. user_dashboard.js serves the user-facing Mini App dashboard where users manage their tracked products. crm_dashboard.js renders the admin CRM dashboard with embedded HTML for managing the entire system. Together, these routes translate HTTP requests into business logic and UI responses.

tour.title: Database Access Layer
tour.description: The database module (src/core/db.js) is the most widely depended-upon file in the project with a fan-in of 5. Every route handler and every worker ultimately goes through this module to read and write data to Cloudflare D1 SQL. Understanding this layer is essential because it defines the data access patterns that the entire application relies on, from user management to product tracking and price history.

tour.title: Telegram Bot Integration
tour.description: The Telegram module (src/core/telegram.js) wraps the Telegram Bot API and is called directly by the entry point and the queue worker. It handles sending notifications to users when product prices change, formatting messages with price drop alerts, and managing bot interactions. This module is the bridge between the price tracking engine and the end user's Telegram client.

tour.title: Internationalization and Localization
tour.description: The i18n module (src/core/i18n.js) provides full localization support for both English and Egyptian Arabic. It is imported by both the CRM dashboard and the user dashboard, ensuring that every user-facing string is rendered in the correct language. This module reflects AzTracker's target audience in Egypt and demonstrates how a serverless application can support multiple languages without a separate translation service.

tour.title: Shared Utilities
tour.description: The utilities module (src/core/utils.js) contains shared helper functions used across the application. The CRM dashboard imports these utilities for common operations like formatting, validation, and data transformation. Keeping these functions in a dedicated module avoids duplication and ensures consistent behavior across different parts of the codebase.

tour.title: Price Scraping Engine
tour.description: The scraper engine (src/workers/scraper_engine.js) is the heart of the price tracking system. It fetches product data from Amazon.eg using access tokens managed by amazon.js, parses the responses at the edge, and stores results via the database layer. It includes a circuit breaker pattern to handle failures gracefully and avoid overwhelming the target site. This module is invoked by the cron trigger on a scheduled basis.
tour.languageLesson: The circuit breaker pattern prevents cascading failures by stopping requests to a failing service after a threshold of errors. It transitions between closed (normal), open (blocking), and half-open (testing recovery) states -- essential for resilient scraping against rate-limited e-commerce sites.

tour.title: Scheduled Triggers and Queue Workers
tour.description: The cron trigger (src/workers/cron_trigger.js) runs on a schedule to initiate price scraping by invoking the scraper engine. The queue worker (src/workers/queue_worker.js) processes individual product updates from Cloudflare Queues, using the Amazon API, database, and Telegram modules to fetch prices, persist changes, and notify users. Together, these workers implement the asynchronous, queue-based architecture that makes AzTracker scalable without running expensive always-on infrastructure.

tour.title: Database Schema
tour.description: The Users table in dump.sql defines the core data model for user accounts. This schema underpins the database access layer from Step 4 and the user-related operations in both dashboards from Step 3. Understanding the table structure clarifies how the application stores user preferences, tracked products, and notification settings in Cloudflare D1 SQL.
tour.languageLesson: Cloudflare D1 is a serverless SQLite database. Schema design in D1 follows standard SQL conventions but must account for the distributed, eventually-consistent nature of the Workers runtime. Foreign keys and indexes should be chosen carefully to minimize read latency.

tour.title: Cloudflare Workers Configuration
tour.description: The wrangler.toml file configures the Cloudflare Workers runtime, defining environment variables, D1 database bindings, KV namespace bindings, and queue consumer/producer settings. This is the infrastructure-as-code file that tells Cloudflare how to deploy and run the worker. The bundled production artifact (dist/index.js) is the compiled output that Wrangler deploys based on this configuration.
tour.languageLesson: Wrangler.toml uses TOML format to define Cloudflare Workers settings. Key sections include [[d1_databases]] for SQL bindings, [[kv_namespaces]] for key-value storage, and [[queues]] for message queue configuration. Environment-specific settings use [env.production] and [env.staging] blocks.

tour.title: CI/CD and Deployment Pipelines
tour.description: The GitHub Actions workflows automate the deployment lifecycle. deploy_worker.yml handles building and deploying the worker to Cloudflare, while sync-prod-to-dev.yml synchronizes production data back to the development environment for safe testing. These pipelines ensure that code changes go through automated testing before reaching production, and that developers always have realistic data to work with.
tour.languageLesson: GitHub Actions workflows use YAML with 'on' triggers (push, schedule, manual), 'jobs' for parallel execution, and 'steps' for sequential commands. The deploy_worker.yml pipeline typically runs 'wrangler deploy' after installing dependencies and running tests.

tour.title: Project Documentation and Roadmap
tour.description: The remaining documentation files provide deeper context. DEPLOYMENT.md covers the step-by-step process for setting up the project in a new environment. TESTING.md explains the testing strategy and how to run tests. ROADMAP.md outlines planned features and the project's future direction. GALLERY.md showcases the UI through screenshots. These files complete the picture of AzTracker as a living, well-documented open-source project.

tour.title: Cloudflare Worker Entry Point
tour.description: src/index.js is the main entry point of the Cloudflare Worker. It wires together all route handlers (CRM dashboard, Telegram webhook, user dashboard) and background workers (cron trigger, queue worker) into a single fetch event listener. This file gives you the bird's-eye view of the entire application runtime -- every request flows through here first.
tour.languageLesson: Cloudflare Workers use a fetch event listener pattern instead of a traditional server.listen(). The export default { fetch(request, env, ctx) } signature is the standard entry point, where env provides access to KV, D1, and Queue bindings configured in wrangler.toml.

tour.title: Platform Configuration
tour.description: Before diving into the code, understand the deployment configuration. wrangler.toml defines the D1 database bindings, queue bindings, environment variables, and cron schedules that power the serverless infrastructure. package.json holds the project metadata and dependencies. Together, these files define the contract between the code and the Cloudflare platform.
tour.languageLesson: wrangler.toml is the Cloudflare Workers configuration file. It binds D1 databases, KV namespaces, and Queues to named environment variables that become available in the Worker's env parameter at runtime. Cron triggers defined here schedule background executions.

tour.title: CRM Admin Dashboard Route
tour.description: src/routes/crm_dashboard.js is the most complex route handler in the project (fan-out of 7, the highest in the codebase). It renders an interactive Admin CRM WebApp directly at the edge, giving administrators full control over tracked products, users, and deals. It imports nearly every core module -- Amazon API, database, i18n, Telegram, utilities, and the scraper engine -- making it the central orchestration point for admin operations.

tour.title: Telegram Webhook and User Dashboard
tour.description: Two complementary routes handle user interaction. src/routes/telegram_webhook.js processes incoming Telegram bot commands and callback queries, acting as the bridge between Telegram users and the backend. src/routes/user_dashboard.js serves a user-facing WebApp dashboard for managing tracked products. Both routes reuse the localization engine (i18n.js) and database layer (db.js), demonstrating the shared-core architecture.

tour.title: Core Services -- Database, Telegram, Amazon API
tour.description: The core layer contains the foundational modules that every route and worker depends on. src/core/db.js provides the D1 database access layer with prepared statements for all SQL operations. src/core/telegram.js wraps the Telegram Bot API for sending messages and managing bot interactions. src/core/amazon.js integrates with the Amazon Creators API and includes an edge parser for product data. These three modules have the highest fan-in in the codebase, meaning they are imported by nearly everything.

tour.title: Localization and Shared Utilities
tour.description: src/core/i18n.js is the most widely imported module (fan-in of 5) because every user-facing component needs it. It provides full localization for English and Egyptian Arabic across all UI surfaces. src/core/utils.js provides shared helper functions used throughout the codebase. Together these modules reduce duplication and enforce consistent behavior across routes and workers.
tour.languageLesson: i18n (internationalization) at the edge: rather than relying on browser language detection or separate URL paths, AzTracker's localization engine resolves the user's locale (English or Egyptian Arabic) from their Telegram user profile or explicit preference, then serves fully translated HTML from the Cloudflare edge.

tour.title: Background Workers -- Cron and Queues
tour.description: AzTracker runs two background workers that operate independently from HTTP request handling. src/workers/cron_trigger.js handles scheduled maintenance tasks and triggers price scraping cycles. src/workers/queue_worker.js processes items from a Cloudflare Queue to deliver outgoing Telegram messages asynchronously, decoupling message sending from user requests for better responsiveness.

tour.title: Scraping Engine with AIMD Governor
tour.description: src/workers/scraper_engine.js is the heart of the price tracking system. It implements a core scraping engine with an AIMD (Additive Increase, Multiplicative Decrease) governor that dynamically adjusts scraping frequency based on product volatility and system load. It also includes a circuit breaker pattern to prevent overwhelming target servers. This module integrates with the Amazon API client (amazon.js) and the localization engine for multi-language content.
tour.languageLesson: The AIMD governor is borrowed from TCP congestion control: scraping frequency increases additively when all requests succeed, but decreases multiplicatively when failures occur. This naturally adapts to product volatility -- frequently changing prices get checked more often, while stable products consume fewer resources.

tour.title: CI/CD and Deployment
tour.description: The GitHub Actions workflow in .github/workflows/deploy_worker.yml automates the deployment pipeline. It runs tests, builds the worker, and deploys to Cloudflare using Wrangler. This file represents the final link in the development chain -- from code commit to production Cloudflare edge deployment.
tour.languageLesson: GitHub Actions workflows for Cloudflare Workers typically use the wrangler-action to authenticate via API token and deploy. The workflow runs on push to main, ensuring that every merged change is automatically deployed to the global Cloudflare network.

tour.title: Architecture Roadmap and Future Plans
tour.description: docs/ROADMAP.md provides the forward-looking perspective on AzTracker's architecture, documenting planned features, technical debt, and design decisions. Pair this with docs/DEPLOYMENT.md for operational details on how the system is staged and deployed to production. Together these documents complete the picture of where the project has been and where it is heading.
